g.data-narration: This lesson covers Doubly Linked Lists in Data Structures
A doubly linked list has pointers to both next and previous nodes enabling bidirectional traversal
g.text: What is a Doubly Linked List?
A linked list where each node contains data,
a pointer to the next node, AND a pointer to
the previous node. Enables bidirectional traversal.

g.data-narration: Each node contains data, a next pointer, and a prev pointer
The visual shows three nodes connected with next and prev pointers forming a bidirectional chain
Advantages include bidirectional traversal and O(1) node deletion but uses more memory
g.text: ✓ Delete node in O(1) with pointer
✓ Delete from end in O(1)* (with tail)
*List needs tail pointer for O(1) end operations

g.data-narration: Create node allocates memory and initializes all pointers to NULL in constant time
g.text: new→data = data; new→next = NULL; new→prev = NULL;

g.data-narration: Insert at beginning updates new node's next to head and head's prev in O(1) time
Insert at end requires a tail pointer for O(1) otherwise requires O(n) traversal
g.text: Insert at End - O(1) with tail, O(n) without

g.data-narration: Insert at position requires updating all four pointers: new next, new prev, and adjacent nodes
Delete from beginning simply moves head pointer and updates prev in O(1) time
Delete from end with tail pointer updates tail and frees memory in O(1) time
g.text: Delete from End - O(1) with tail

g.data-narration: Delete given node is O(1) using prev and next pointers unlike singly linked list O(n)
g.text: Delete Given Node - O(1) - Major Advantage!

g.data-narration: Reverse traversal is easy using prev pointers unlike singly requiring a stack
Reverse the list by swapping prev and next pointers for each node in O(n) time
O(1) operations include insert/delete at beginning and delete node; O(n) for position operations
Applications include browser history and music players; key is trading 50% more memory for bidirectional access
